name: Fushimi Inari-taisha
description: Ten thousand vermilion torii gates wind up the forested slopes of Mount Inari. The full hike to the summit takes about two hours and passes dozens of sub-shrines with panoramic city views.

name: Kinkaku-ji (Golden Pavilion)
description: Japan's most photographed building — a three-story Zen temple sheathed in gold leaf, perfectly mirrored in the still waters of the surrounding Kyoko-chi pond.

name: Kiyomizu-dera Temple
description: A UNESCO World Heritage temple perched on a hillside above eastern Kyoto. Its wooden stage juts 13 metres over the treetops, offering sweeping views of the city and Kyoto Tower.

name: Gion District
description: Kyoto's most famous geisha district — preserved machiya townhouses, stone-paved Hanamikoji-dori, lantern-lit tea houses, and the chance to spot a geiko or maiko at dusk.

name: Arashiyama Bamboo Grove
description: A corridor of towering bamboo stalks lines the path from Tenryu-ji to Nonomiya Shrine. Best visited at opening time to experience the eerie rustling in near-solitude.

name: Nijo Castle
description: The 17th-century Tokugawa shogunate palace, famed for 'nightingale floors' that squeak to alert against intruders. Lavish painted sliding screens and a moat-encircled garden.

name: Nishiki Market
description: A narrow 400-year-old covered arcade with 100+ stalls selling Kyoto pickles, fresh tofu, grilled skewers, matcha sweets, and every regional delicacy the city is known for.

name: Philosopher's Path
description: A stone-paved canal-side walkway linking Ginkaku-ji to Nanzen-ji, lined with hundreds of cherry trees and quiet cafés. Named after philosopher Nishida Kitaro's daily contemplative walks.

name: Ryoan-ji Rock Garden
description: Home to Japan's most famous karesansui garden — 15 stones arranged in white gravel so that only 14 are ever visible from any single viewpoint. A UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Ginkaku-ji (Silver Pavilion)
description: Despite its name, the pavilion was never covered in silver. The real draw is the Higashiyama-jishi sand cone and wave-raked dry garden of this supremely restrained Zen complex.

name: Tenryu-ji Garden
description: A masterpiece of Zen garden design by Musō Soseki, with a pond that mirrors the Arashiyama mountains. The 14th-century sōgen garden is a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

name: Sanjusangendo
description: Japan's longest wooden structure stretches 120m and houses 1,001 gilded Kannon statues — each with 11 heads and 40 arms — flanking a singular seated deity in solemn rows.

name: Pontocho Alley
description: A narrow stone-paved alley running between the Kamo River and Kiyamachi-dori, lined with kaiseki restaurants, yakitori bars, and intimate cocktail lounges.

name: Tofuku-ji Temple
description: One of Kyoto's great Rinzai Zen monasteries — famous for Shigemori Mirei's modernist checkerboard stone-and-moss gardens and the largest sanmon gate of any Japanese Zen temple.

name: Kyoto National Museum
description: Founded in 1897, the museum holds over 12,500 artifacts — Buddhist sculpture, ink paintings, lacquerware, and screens — sourced from Kyoto's greatest temples and shrines.

name: Daitoku-ji Temple Complex
description: A sprawling Rinzai Zen complex of 24 sub-temples, many with exquisite private gardens. Historically central to the tea ceremony — Sen no Rikyu had his own sub-temple here.

name: Kyoto Imperial Palace Park
description: Sprawling grounds surrounding the former imperial residence, now a public park with cherry blossoms, gravel paths, and free guided palace tours. A tranquil oasis in central Kyoto.

name: Fushimi Sake District
description: One of Japan's most celebrated sake-brewing regions. Centuries-old kura line the Fushimi Momoyama canal — most offer tours and tasting sessions of their signature junmai.

name: Maruyama Park
description: Kyoto's most popular public park, centred on a magnificent weeping cherry tree. Street food stalls line the paths and the park opens directly into Yasaka Shrine.

name: Nanzen-ji Temple & Aqueduct
description: A grand Rinzai Zen head temple famous for its soaring sanmon gate and a striking 19th-century brick aqueduct cutting through the grounds — one of Kyoto's most photogenic contrasts.

name: Heian Shrine
description: A large Shinto shrine built in 1895 to commemorate Kyoto's 1,100th anniversary. The sweeping vermilion torii gate, Chinese-influenced main hall, and stroll garden of seasonal blooms are all landmark sights.

name: Byodoin Temple
description: An 11th-century temple in Uji whose Phoenix Hall is so revered it appears on the 10-yen coin. The gilded Amida Buddha and elegant pond garden are among the finest surviving examples of Heian-era design.

name: To-ji Temple
description: Home to Japan's tallest pagoda at 55m, To-ji was founded in 796 and given to the monk Kukai to establish Esoteric Buddhism. A vast flea market fills the grounds on the 21st of each month.

name: Ninenzaka & Sannenzaka
description: Kyoto's most photogenic preserved streetscape — cobblestoned stone lanes lined with centuries-old machiya converted into matcha shops, ceramics studios, textiles, and Kyoto craft boutiques.

name: Yasaka Shrine
description: The guardian shrine of Gion, open 24 hours with lantern-lit stone paths and an ornate vermilion-and-white main hall. Host of the Gion Matsuri — Japan's most famous festival — every July.

name: Toei Kyoto Studio Park
description: A working film studio and theme park in Uzumasa where samurai dramas, ninja films, and anime have been made for decades. Dress as a ninja or geisha, watch stunt shows, and explore Edo-period street sets.

name: Kyoto International Manga Museum
nameJa: 京都国際マンガミュージアム
description: Housed in a converted primary school, the museum holds 300,000 manga volumes spanning 130 years of Japanese comics. Visitors read freely on the lawn — there are also rotating exhibitions and live artist sessions.

name: Ninnaji Temple
description: A UNESCO World Heritage temple founded in 888 by Emperor Uda. Known for its short, late-blooming Omuro cherry trees — some over 120 years old — and a classic five-story pagoda in a quiet northwest neighbourhood.

name: Arashiyama & Togetsukyo Bridge
description: The scenic heart of Arashiyama, where the Oi River flows beneath a wooden bridge backed by forested mountains. Riverside cafés, rental rowboats, and the starting point for the bamboo grove and monkey park.

name: Sagano Scenic Railway
description: A 7.3km open-air sightseeing train that winds through the Hozu River gorge from Arashiyama to Kameoka. Dense maple and cedar forest crowds the canyon walls — spectacular in autumn.

name: Shimogamo Shrine
description: One of Japan's oldest shrines, approached through the Tadasu-no-Mori — a primeval forest of 600-year-old trees. A UNESCO World Heritage Site, with a famous traditional archery ceremony each January.

name: Iwatayama Monkey Park
nameJa: 嵐山モンキーパーク岩田山
description: A short hike above Arashiyama leads to a hilltop habitat of 120 wild Japanese macaques. Feed them through a wire fence at the summit building and enjoy panoramic views over Kyoto.

name: Kyoto Tower
description: A 131m observation tower designed to evoke a candle flame, directly opposite Kyoto Station. The only tall structure on the Kyoto skyline, its deck provides a 360° view contrasting ancient temple rooftops with modern streets.

name: Chion-in Temple
description: Headquarters of the Jodo Buddhist sect, with Japan's largest sanmon gate and its heaviest bell at 70 tonnes. The white-walled corridors and moss gardens feel austere and commanding next to the busier Gion district.

name: Kurama Temple & Mount Kurama
description: A remote mountain temple complex reached by cable car or a forested hiking trail north of Kyoto. The cedar-covered ridgeline trail connects to Kibune Shrine, with riverside cafés serving nagashi somen in summer.

name: Katsura Imperial Villa
description: Considered the pinnacle of Japanese architectural and garden design, this 17th-century imperial retreat influenced Modernist architects from Bruno Taut to Walter Gropius. Entry requires advance reservation through the Imperial Household Agency.

name: Jonangu Shrine Gardens
description: A little-visited shrine with five exquisite gardens representing the seasons, famous for cascading wisteria in spring and 150 plum trees in February. A tranquil contrast to Kyoto's most crowded sites.

name: Mount Daimonji
description: The wooded peak east of Ginkaku-ji, famous for the Gozan Okuribi fire festival each August — a giant 大 (dai) character blazes on its face to guide ancestral spirits home. Outside festival season, the trail to the 466m summit offers sweeping panoramic views across Kyoto's rooftops and into the mountains beyond.

name: Kyoto Trail
description: An 85km long-distance trail that circles the mountains surrounding Kyoto, passing through Fushimi, Higashiyama, Kurama, and Arashiyama. Most visitors tackle one section — the Higashiyama Course from Fushimi Inari north to Nanzen-ji is the most scenic half-day route, passing Kiyomizudera and Kodai-ji on the way.

name: Kongo Noh Theatre
description: Headquarters of the Kongo school of Noh — one of Japan's five major schools — with a lineage stretching over 700 years. Located near the Imperial Palace in Kamigyo, the theatre hosts monthly subscription performances (定期能) open to the public, along with special seasonal programmes.
